Definition: The service controlling authentication (who are you?) and authorization (what can you do?) in AWS — using users, groups, roles, and JSON policies with Effect/Action/Resource/Condition to enforce least-privilege access across all AWS services.

JSON documents defining permissions — Identity-based (attached to users/groups/roles), Resource-based (attached to resources like S3 buckets/SQS queues), Permission boundaries (max permissions cap), and SCPs (organization-wide guardrails). Evaluation: explicit Deny > Allow > implicit Deny.

Temporary credential sets assumed by AWS services, applications, or users — no long-term credentials, automatically rotated by STS. Used for EC2 instance profiles, Lambda execution roles, cross-account access, and identity federation.

A service for centrally managing multiple AWS accounts — using Organizational Units (OUs) for grouping, Service Control Policies (SCPs) for permission guardrails, consolidated billing for cost savings, and AWS Control Tower for automated governance.

Organization-level policies that set maximum permission boundaries for member accounts — they don't grant permissions but restrict what IAM policies can allow. Applied to OUs or accounts, inherited hierarchically, and don't affect the management account.

A managed service for creating and controlling encryption keys — symmetric (AES-256) and asymmetric (RSA, ECC) CMKs with key policies, automatic annual rotation, CloudTrail audit logging, and envelope encryption for data larger than 4 KB.

Dedicated hardware security modules (HSMs) in the cloud — single-tenant, FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated, you manage your own keys (AWS has no access). Used when regulatory requirements mandate dedicated hardware or you need full key control.

KMS: multi-tenant, AWS manages HSM hardware, integrated with 100+ AWS services, automatic key rotation, lower cost. CloudHSM: single-tenant, you manage keys, FIPS 140-2 Level 3, supports asymmetric keys and custom key stores — choose CloudHSM for strict compliance requirements.

A service that stores, rotates, and retrieves secrets (database credentials, API keys) — with automatic rotation via Lambda, cross-account sharing, RDS/Aurora/Redshift/DocumentDB native rotation support, and SDK integration for runtime retrieval.

A hierarchical key-value store for configuration data and secrets — String, StringList, and SecureString (KMS-encrypted) types. Free standard tier (up to 10,000 parameters), advanced tier for higher throughput and parameter policies with expiration notifications.

A managed identity service — User Pools for authentication (sign-up/sign-in, MFA, social/SAML federation, JWT tokens) and Identity Pools for authorization (temporary AWS credentials for authenticated/guest users to access AWS services directly).

A Layer 7 firewall protecting web applications — attached to CloudFront, ALB, API Gateway, or AppSync with rules for SQL injection, XSS, rate limiting, IP/geo blocking, and AWS Managed Rules. Web ACLs contain prioritized rules evaluated in order.

DDoS protection — Shield Standard (free, automatic Layer 3/4 protection for all AWS resources) and Shield Advanced (paid, enhanced protection for CloudFront/ALB/Route 53/Global Accelerator with 24/7 DDoS Response Team, cost protection, and real-time metrics).

A central security management service for configuring and managing WAF rules, Shield Advanced protections, VPC security groups, and Network Firewall policies across multiple accounts in AWS Organizations — ensuring consistent security posture.

An intelligent threat detection service that continuously monitors AWS accounts — analyzing CloudTrail logs, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs using ML to detect unauthorized access, compromised instances, cryptocurrency mining, and malicious IP communication.

An automated vulnerability assessment service — scanning EC2 instances, Lambda functions, and ECR container images for software vulnerabilities (CVEs), network exposure, and unintended network accessibility with prioritized findings.

A data security service using ML to discover, classify, and protect sensitive data in S3 — automatically detecting PII, financial data, credentials, and intellectual property with alerts and integration with Security Hub.

A centralized security dashboard that aggregates findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, Firewall Manager, and third-party tools — providing a comprehensive security posture view with automated compliance checks against standards (CIS, PCI DSS).

A governance service logging all API calls in your AWS account — recording who, when, from where, and what was requested. Management events (control plane) logged by default; data events (S3 object/Lambda invocations) optional. Stored in S3 with integrity validation.

Stateful virtual firewalls at the instance/ENI level — allow rules only (no deny), evaluate all rules before deciding, return traffic automatically allowed. Default: all outbound allowed, all inbound denied. Reference other security groups for dynamic access control.

Stateless firewalls at the subnet level — support allow AND deny rules, evaluated in numbered order (lowest first), return traffic must be explicitly allowed. Default NACL allows all; custom NACLs deny all by default. Use for subnet-level IP blocking.

Security Groups: stateful, instance-level, allow only, all rules evaluated. NACLs: stateless, subnet-level, allow+deny, rules evaluated in order. Best practice: use Security Groups as primary defense, NACLs as additional subnet-level protection layer.

Private connections from VPC to AWS services without internet — Gateway endpoints (S3, DynamoDB, free, route table entry) and Interface endpoints (most other services, powered by PrivateLink, ENI in subnet, security group controlled, per-hour + per-GB cost).

A technology for private connectivity between VPCs and services — traffic stays on the AWS network, never traversing the internet. Used for accessing AWS services, your own services, and third-party SaaS via interface VPC endpoints.

Server-side: SSE-S3 (Amazon-managed, default, AES-256), SSE-KMS (KMS keys, audit trail, separate permissions, per-request charges), SSE-C (customer-provided keys). Client-side: encrypt before upload. Enforce via bucket policies requiring encryption headers.

Term: S3 Bucket Policies and Access Control
Definition: Resource-based JSON policies for bucket/object access — supporting IP restrictions, VPC endpoint conditions, MFA delete requirements, encryption enforcement, cross-account grants, and S3 Block Public Access (account/bucket level) to prevent accidental public exposure.

Named network endpoints with dedicated access policies for S3 buckets — simplifying access management for shared datasets by creating per-application or per-team access points with specific permissions instead of complex bucket policies.

A service provisioning and managing SSL/TLS certificates — free public certificates that auto-renew, integrated with ALB, CloudFront, API Gateway, and NLB for HTTPS. Private CA available for internal certificates. Cannot export public certificates.

A service that records and evaluates AWS resource configurations over time — tracking configuration changes, evaluating compliance against rules (managed and custom), and enabling remediation actions. Answers: 'What did my resource look like at time X?'

A centralized service for managing SSO access to multiple AWS accounts and business applications — integrating with Active Directory or external IdPs, providing a single user portal, and supporting SAML 2.0 and OIDC federation.

A managed firewall service for VPCs — providing stateful inspection, intrusion prevention (IPS), web filtering, and protocol detection at the network level. Deployed in a dedicated firewall subnet with rules managed centrally via Firewall Manager.

Virtual servers with configurable CPU, memory, storage, and networking — supporting On-Demand, Reserved (1/3 year, up to 72% savings), Spot (up to 90% savings, can be interrupted), Savings Plans, Dedicated Hosts, and Dedicated Instances.

Families optimized for different workloads — General Purpose (M/T: balanced), Compute Optimized (C: batch, HPC, ML inference), Memory Optimized (R/X/z: databases, caches), Storage Optimized (I/D/H: data warehousing, distributed file systems), Accelerated (P/G: GPU, ML training).

Strategies for instance placement — Cluster (low latency, single AZ, HPC), Spread (max 7 per AZ, critical instances on separate hardware), Partition (large distributed workloads like HDFS/Cassandra, instances in logical partitions on separate racks).

Automatically adjusting EC2 capacity based on demand — using launch templates, scaling policies (target tracking, step, simple, scheduled, predictive), health checks, and cooldown periods to maintain performance and minimize costs.

Target Tracking (maintain metric at target, e.g., CPU at 50%), Step Scaling (scale by different amounts based on alarm thresholds), Simple Scaling (single adjustment, waits for cooldown), Scheduled (time-based), Predictive (ML-based forecasting).

Distributing traffic across targets — ALB (Layer 7, HTTP/HTTPS, path/host routing, WebSocket), NLB (Layer 4, TCP/UDP/TLS, ultra-low latency, static IP, millions of requests/sec), GLB (Layer 3, third-party virtual appliances), CLB (legacy).

Layer 7 load balancer for HTTP/HTTPS — supporting path-based routing (/api, /images), host-based routing (api.example.com), query string routing, weighted target groups, Lambda targets, authentication (Cognito/OIDC), sticky sessions, and WebSocket.

Layer 4 load balancer for TCP/UDP/TLS — ultra-low latency (millions of requests/sec), static IP per AZ (or Elastic IP), preserves source IP, supports PrivateLink, and handles volatile/spiky traffic patterns. Ideal for gaming, IoT, and financial applications.

Distributing traffic evenly across all registered targets in all AZs — regardless of AZ distribution. Enabled by default for ALB (free), optional for NLB (charges apply). Prevents uneven load when AZs have different numbers of targets.

The time ELB waits for in-flight requests to complete before deregistering an unhealthy/deregistering target — default 300 seconds, configurable 0-3600. Set lower for short requests, higher for long-running uploads or queries.

A highly available DNS service — supporting routing policies: Simple, Weighted (traffic splitting), Latency-based (nearest region), Failover (active-passive DR), Geolocation (country/continent), Geoproximity (bias-adjustable), and Multi-value (up to 8 healthy records).

Monitoring endpoint health for DNS failover — HTTP/HTTPS/TCP checks every 10 or 30 seconds from global locations. Calculated health checks combine multiple checks. CloudWatch alarm-based checks for private resources. Unhealthy endpoints removed from DNS responses.

Tiered storage for cost optimization — S3 Standard (frequent access), S3 Intelligent-Tiering (auto-tiering), S3 Standard-IA (infrequent, 30-day min), S3 One Zone-IA (single AZ, 30-day min), S3 Glacier Instant/Flexible/Deep Archive (archival, minutes to 12 hours retrieval).

Rules that automatically transition objects between storage classes or expire them — e.g., move to Standard-IA after 30 days, Glacier after 90 days, delete after 365 days. Reduces costs by matching storage class to access patterns over time.

Keeping multiple variants of an object in the same bucket — protecting against accidental deletion (delete markers instead of permanent deletion) and overwrites. MFA Delete adds extra protection requiring MFA to permanently delete versions or disable versioning.

Cross-Region Replication (CRR) for compliance, lower latency, and DR. Same-Region Replication (SRR) for log aggregation and live replication between accounts. Requires versioning enabled, supports different storage classes and accounts, and replicates new objects (not retroactive).

Persistent block storage for EC2 — gp3/gp2 (general purpose SSD, boot volumes), io2/io1 (provisioned IOPS SSD, databases), st1 (throughput HDD, big data), sc1 (cold HDD, infrequent). Single AZ, snapshots to S3 for backup/migration.

gp3: 3,000 baseline IOPS, up to 16,000, independent throughput. gp2: 3 IOPS/GB, burst to 3,000. io2 Block Express: up to 256,000 IOPS, 99.999% durability. io2/io1: up to 64,000 IOPS. st1: throughput-optimized 500 MB/s. sc1: cold storage 250 MB/s.

Point-in-time backups stored in S3 — incremental (only changed blocks), can create volumes in any AZ from a snapshot, copy across regions for DR, share with other accounts, and create AMIs. Fast Snapshot Restore (FSR) eliminates latency on first access.

A managed NFS file system for Linux — shared across multiple EC2 instances and AZs simultaneously, auto-scaling from GB to PB, supporting Standard and Infrequent Access storage classes with lifecycle policies. POSIX-compliant, supports NFSv4.

Managed file systems — FSx for Windows File Server (SMB, Active Directory, NTFS), FSx for Lustre (HPC, ML training, S3 integration), FSx for NetApp ONTAP (multi-protocol NFS/SMB/iSCSI), FSx for OpenZFS (Linux workloads, snapshots).

EBS: single instance (except io1/io2 multi-attach), single AZ, block storage, boot volumes. EFS: multi-instance, multi-AZ, NFS, Linux only, auto-scaling. S3: object storage, unlimited, serverless, highest durability — choose based on access pattern and sharing needs.

Managed relational databases — MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server, and Aurora. Handles provisioning, patching, backups, recovery, and scaling. Multi-AZ for HA (synchronous standby), Read Replicas for read scaling (async replication).

A MySQL/PostgreSQL-compatible managed database — up to 5x MySQL and 3x PostgreSQL performance, 6 copies of data across 3 AZs, auto-scaling storage (10 GB to 128 TB), up to 15 read replicas with sub-10ms lag, and automatic failover in under 30 seconds.

An on-demand auto-scaling Aurora configuration — automatically starts, scales, and shuts down based on application needs. V2 scales instantly in fine-grained increments. Ideal for infrequent, intermittent, or unpredictable workloads. Pay per ACU-second.

A feature spanning up to 5 secondary regions with sub-second replication — enabling low-latency global reads and disaster recovery with RPO of 1 second and RTO under 1 minute via managed planned failover or unplanned cross-region failover.

High availability deployment — synchronous replication to a standby in another AZ, automatic failover (60-120 seconds) on primary failure, same DNS endpoint. Multi-AZ DB Cluster (new): one writer + two readable standbys across 3 AZs for read scaling + HA.

Asynchronous read copies for scaling read workloads — up to 5 replicas (15 for Aurora), can be cross-AZ or cross-region, promotable to standalone DB. Cross-region replicas useful for DR and global read performance. Read replicas have their own endpoint.

A fully managed serverless NoSQL database — single-digit millisecond performance at any scale, on-demand or provisioned capacity, auto-scaling, global tables (multi-region, multi-active), DAX caching, streams, TTL, and point-in-time recovery.

Multi-region, multi-active replication — enabling low-latency reads and writes in any region with automatic conflict resolution (last writer wins). Requires DynamoDB Streams enabled, provides RPO/RTO of seconds for regional disaster recovery.

Managed in-memory caching — Redis (persistence, replication, pub/sub, sorted sets, Multi-AZ, backup/restore) and Memcached (multi-threaded, simple, no persistence). Use for database caching, session stores, leaderboards, and real-time analytics.

Backup & Restore (RPO hours, RTO hours, lowest cost), Pilot Light (RPO minutes, RTO tens of minutes, core services running), Warm Standby (RPO seconds, RTO minutes, scaled-down full environment), Multi-Site Active-Active (RPO near-zero, RTO near-zero, highest cost).

A centralized backup service for EC2, EBS, RDS, DynamoDB, EFS, FSx, S3, and more — with backup plans (schedules, retention, lifecycle), cross-region/cross-account backup, vault lock for compliance (WORM), and backup audit manager.

An isolated virtual network — with subnets (public/private across AZs), route tables, internet gateway (public access), NAT gateway (private subnet outbound), VPC peering (non-transitive), Transit Gateway (hub-and-spoke), and VPN/Direct Connect for hybrid connectivity.

A networking connection between two VPCs enabling private IP communication — non-transitive (A↔B and B↔C doesn't mean A↔C), no overlapping CIDRs, works cross-account and cross-region. For many VPCs, use Transit Gateway instead.

A hub-and-spoke network transit hub — connecting VPCs, VPNs, Direct Connect, and peered Transit Gateways through a single gateway. Supports transitive routing, route tables, multicast, and inter-region peering. Simplifies complex network topologies.

A dedicated private network connection from on-premises to AWS — 1 Gbps or 10 Gbps dedicated connections, or 50 Mbps to 10 Gbps hosted connections. Lower latency and consistent throughput vs internet. Takes weeks to establish; use VPN as backup.

An encrypted IPsec tunnel between on-premises and AWS VPC — over the public internet, quick to set up (minutes), supports BGP for dynamic routing. Use as primary connection for non-critical workloads or as backup for Direct Connect.

Direct Connect (primary, dedicated, consistent) + Site-to-Site VPN (backup, encrypted, quick setup). Direct Connect Gateway for multi-region access. Transit Gateway for connecting multiple VPCs and on-premises networks through a single hub.

A global CDN with 400+ edge locations — caching content close to users for low latency, supporting static/dynamic content, HTTPS, Lambda@Edge/CloudFront Functions for edge compute, Origin Access Control (OAC) for S3, and custom/S3/ALB/MediaStore origins.

A feature restricting S3 bucket access to only CloudFront — replacing the legacy Origin Access Identity (OAI). Ensures users cannot bypass CloudFront to access S3 directly, supporting SSE-KMS encryption and all S3 regions.

Rules determining how CloudFront handles requests — path patterns (/images/*, /api/*), TTL settings, allowed HTTP methods, viewer protocol policy (HTTPS redirect), cache keys (headers, cookies, query strings), and origin selection.

Lambda@Edge: Node.js/Python, runs at regional edge caches, up to 10 seconds (viewer) or 30 seconds (origin), access to network/file system. CloudFront Functions: JavaScript only, runs at all edge locations, sub-millisecond, lightweight (header manipulation, URL rewrites, redirects).

A networking service routing traffic through AWS global network to optimal endpoints — providing static Anycast IPs, automatic failover between regions, and up to 60% performance improvement. Unlike CloudFront (caches content), Global Accelerator optimizes the network path.

CloudFront: content caching at edge, HTTP/HTTPS, ideal for static/dynamic web content. Global Accelerator: network-level optimization, TCP/UDP, static IPs, ideal for non-HTTP (gaming, IoT, VoIP) and applications requiring deterministic routing and instant failover.

Speeds up S3 uploads by routing through CloudFront edge locations to S3 via optimized AWS network paths — beneficial for long-distance transfers (cross-continent uploads), large objects, and users distributed globally.

3,500 PUT/COPY/POST/DELETE and 5,500 GET/HEAD requests per second per prefix — use multiple prefixes for parallelism, multipart upload for large files (required >5 GB), byte-range fetches for parallel downloads, and S3 Select for retrieving subsets of data.

A fully managed message queue — Standard (unlimited throughput, at-least-once, best-effort ordering) and FIFO (300 TPS/3,000 with batching, exactly-once, strict ordering). Decouples components, with visibility timeout, DLQ, long polling, and message retention up to 14 days.

A fully managed pub/sub messaging service — publishers send to topics, subscribers (SQS, Lambda, HTTP, email, SMS, Kinesis Firehose) receive copies. Supports message filtering, FIFO topics, and fan-out to multiple SQS queues for parallel processing.

Real-time data streaming — Kinesis Data Streams (custom processing, 24h-365d retention), Kinesis Data Firehose (managed delivery to S3/Redshift/OpenSearch), Kinesis Data Analytics (SQL/Flink processing), Kinesis Video Streams (video ingestion and processing).

SQS: pull-based queue, consumer deletes messages, decoupling. SNS: push-based pub/sub, fan-out to multiple subscribers. Kinesis: real-time streaming, multiple consumers read same data, replay capability, ordering per shard — choose based on pattern (queue vs pub/sub vs stream).

Serverless compute running code in response to events — up to 15 minutes execution, 10 GB memory, 10 GB /tmp storage, 1,000 concurrent executions (default). Pay per request and compute duration. Integrates with 200+ AWS services as event sources.

A managed service for REST, HTTP, and WebSocket APIs — with throttling (10,000 RPS default), caching, authentication (IAM/Cognito/Lambda authorizer), request/response transformation, usage plans, and 29-second integration timeout.

Serverless workflow orchestration — coordinating Lambda functions and AWS services using state machines with sequential, parallel, branching, error handling, retry, wait, and Map states. Standard (1 year, exactly-once) and Express (5 min, at-least-once) workflows.

A serverless event bus — routing events from AWS services, SaaS apps, and custom sources to targets based on rules and patterns. Features: schema registry, archive/replay, cross-account/cross-region event delivery, and scheduled rules (cron/rate).

A petabyte-scale columnar data warehouse — using massively parallel processing (MPP), columnar storage, and result caching for fast analytics. Redshift Spectrum queries S3 data directly. Supports RA3 nodes (managed storage), Serverless, and cross-region snapshots.

A serverless ETL service — Glue Crawlers discover schema and populate the Data Catalog, Glue Jobs run Spark-based transformations, and Glue Studio provides visual ETL authoring. Integrates with Athena, Redshift, and EMR for analytics pipelines.

A serverless SQL query engine for S3 — pay per query ($5/TB scanned), supports CSV, JSON, Parquet, ORC, Avro. Use columnar formats (Parquet/ORC) and partitioning to reduce costs and improve performance. Integrates with Glue Data Catalog for schema.

A managed search and analytics engine (successor to Elasticsearch) — for log analytics, full-text search, application monitoring, and clickstream analytics. Supports OpenSearch Dashboards for visualization and integrates with Kinesis Firehose for log ingestion.

A managed big data platform — running Apache Spark, Hive, HBase, Presto, and Flink on EC2 or EKS. Supports Spot instances for cost savings, auto-scaling, and EMRFS for S3 integration. Used for ETL, log analysis, ML, and genomics processing.

A service for building secure data lakes on S3 — centralizing data governance with fine-grained access control (column/row/cell level), data cataloging, ETL job creation, and cross-account data sharing via LF-Tags.

S3 (data lake storage) → Glue (ETL/catalog) → Athena (ad-hoc SQL) / Redshift (warehouse) / EMR (big data processing) → QuickSight (visualization). Kinesis for real-time ingestion, Lake Formation for governance.

A managed container orchestration service — running Docker containers on EC2 (you manage instances) or Fargate (serverless, pay per task). Task definitions specify containers, and services maintain desired count with ALB/NLB integration and auto-scaling.

A managed Kubernetes service — running Kubernetes control plane across multiple AZs, supporting EC2 and Fargate node types, Kubernetes-native tooling, and hybrid deployment with EKS Anywhere. Choose EKS when you need Kubernetes compatibility.

A serverless compute engine for containers (ECS and EKS) — no EC2 instance management, pay per vCPU and memory per second, automatic scaling, and task-level isolation. Ideal when you want containers without managing infrastructure.

An Aurora feature where all DB instances can perform read and write operations — providing continuous write availability even if a writer instance fails. Used for applications requiring 100% write uptime across multiple AZs.

On-Demand (pay per second/hour, no commitment), Reserved Instances (1/3 year, up to 72% savings, Standard/Convertible), Savings Plans (flexible, up to 72%, Compute/EC2/SageMaker), Spot (up to 90% savings, can be interrupted with 2-min notice), Dedicated Hosts (per-host billing, BYOL).

Spare EC2 capacity at up to 90% discount — can be interrupted with 2-minute warning. Best for fault-tolerant workloads: batch processing, data analysis, CI/CD, containerized workloads. Use Spot Fleet or Auto Scaling mixed instances for availability.

RIs: specific instance type/region/OS, Standard (exchange limited) or Convertible (flexible). Savings Plans: Compute SP (any instance, region, OS, Lambda, Fargate), EC2 Instance SP (specific family/region). Savings Plans offer more flexibility at similar discounts.

An ML-powered service that analyzes utilization metrics and recommends optimal EC2 instance types, EBS volumes, Lambda memory, and ECS/Fargate configurations — identifying over-provisioned and under-provisioned resources for cost and performance optimization.

Use appropriate storage classes (Standard → IA → Glacier), lifecycle policies for automatic transitions, S3 Intelligent-Tiering for unknown patterns, S3 Analytics for access pattern insights, and S3 Inventory for auditing. Compress and use columnar formats to reduce storage.

An automatic storage class that moves objects between tiers based on access patterns — Frequent, Infrequent (30 days), Archive Instant (90 days), Archive (90-730 days), Deep Archive (180-730 days). No retrieval fees, small monthly monitoring fee per object.

Inbound to AWS is free. Outbound to internet is charged per GB (tiered pricing). Between AZs: ~$0.01/GB each way. Between regions: $0.02/GB. Use VPC endpoints, CloudFront, same-AZ placement, and S3 gateway endpoints to minimize transfer costs.

A visualization tool for analyzing AWS spending — viewing cost and usage over time, forecasting future costs, identifying cost drivers, and discovering savings opportunities with Reserved Instance and Savings Plan recommendations.

Setting custom cost and usage budgets with alerts — budget types: cost, usage, reservation utilization, and Savings Plans utilization. Alerts via email or SNS when actual or forecasted spending exceeds thresholds. Supports automated actions (stop instances, apply SCPs).

The most detailed cost dataset — line-item billing data delivered to S3, queryable with Athena or loaded into Redshift. Includes resource IDs, tags, pricing, and usage for granular cost allocation and chargeback analysis.

Matching instance types and sizes to workload requirements — using CloudWatch metrics, Compute Optimizer recommendations, and Trusted Advisor checks to identify underutilized resources and downsize or terminate them for cost savings.

Pay-per-use pricing with no idle costs — Lambda (per request + duration), DynamoDB On-Demand (per read/write), API Gateway (per request), S3 (per storage + request), Fargate (per vCPU + memory). Ideal for variable/unpredictable workloads.

Reserved Instances for steady-state databases, Aurora Serverless for intermittent workloads, read replicas to offload reads from expensive primary, appropriate instance sizing, and stopping non-production databases during off-hours (max 7 days stopped).

Use gp3 over gp2 (20% cheaper baseline, independent IOPS/throughput), delete unattached volumes, use snapshots instead of keeping idle volumes, transition old snapshots to archive tier (75% cheaper), and right-size provisioned IOPS volumes.

NAT Gateway: managed, highly available per AZ, scales to 100 Gbps, ~$0.045/hour + $0.045/GB. NAT Instance: self-managed EC2, can use as bastion, cheaper for low traffic (t3.micro), supports port forwarding. Use NAT Gateway for production, NAT Instance for dev/test cost savings.

Inspects your AWS environment across 5 pillars — Cost Optimization (idle resources, underutilized), Performance (overutilized, service limits), Security (open ports, IAM), Fault Tolerance (backups, Multi-AZ), Service Limits. Full checks require Business/Enterprise Support.

Key-value metadata attached to AWS resources — enabling cost allocation (tag-based billing reports), access control (IAM tag conditions), automation (tag-based Lambda triggers), and organization. Implement a consistent tagging strategy for governance.
